context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out this reflection piece on development theories and the global crisis. I’m honestly a bit stuck on the first question about whether “development” still has a core meaning or if it’s become too broad. Could you help me narrow that down?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Before we dig in — what do *you* think 'development' means? Just off the top of your head, no need to reference readings yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help narrowing down the first question. Rather than explaining the concept or giving scaffolding, I should use the Socratic method and ask them what they already think, which will help them engage with their own reasoning first. This keeps the student doing the intellectual work while I guide the direction.

exchanges.student: My first instinct is that development means improving people’s quality of life, not just growing the economy. But I’m already unsure because in this course it also seems tied to power, the state, institutions, and even who gets to define “improvement.” I think I get the general idea, but I’m not sure how to say it without making it way too vague.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually put your finger on something important. You said development is about 'improving quality of life' — but then immediately noted that who defines 'improvement' is contested. So what does that tension suggest to you about whether development has a shared, agreed-upon meaning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown good initial thinking - they've identified a tension between a broad humanistic definition and the more complex political/institutional dimensions. This is actually very close to the core of the question. Rather than validating and moving on, I should push them to confront the tension they've identified, as that tension IS the substance of the first question in the prompt. I'll ask them to think about what that tension implies for whether 'development' has an agreed-upon meaning.

exchanges.student: I guess it suggests there’s maybe a core idea, but not a fully agreed version of it. Like, a lot of people might agree development is about improving human well-being, but they don’t agree on the path, the priorities, or who decides. Is that too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid distinction — thin consensus on goals, deep disagreement on means and agency. Now push it a bit further: the prompt asks specifically if development has become *meaningless* because it's too all-inclusive. Does your reasoning support that claim, or does it push back against it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced and defensible position - that there's a thin consensus around well-being but deep disagreement about means, priorities, and agency. This is actually a strong foundation for their reflection piece. I should affirm the direction without praising excessively, and push them to connect this to the second part of the first question: has it become 'all-inclusive and therefore meaningless'? That's the sharper edge of the prompt they haven't fully addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I think it pushes back against it, at least partly. If there’s still some basic shared concern with well-being, dignity, poverty reduction, or expanding opportunities, then it’s not meaningless. But I can also see why people say it gets fuzzy, because it can include almost everything from GDP to rights to sustainability to participation, and that makes it harder to pin down.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're holding that tension well. Now, that naturally leads to the second question: if development means different things to different actors, what does *success* look like? And can you think of any country or community that might be considered a role model — and why that choice might itself be controversial?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a well-balanced argument. They're holding the tension well between 'not meaningless' and 'genuinely fuzzy.' This is good progress. Rather than dwelling further on this first question, I should nudge them toward the second question about success and role models, which connects naturally to what they've just said. If development means different things, then what counts as success also becomes contested - that's the logical next step.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking success probably can’t just mean fast growth anymore. Maybe it means some mix of economic improvement, reduced inequality, stronger institutions, and people having more real control over their lives. For role models, I thought of South Korea at first because of industrialization, but then that feels too simple since it had authoritarian periods and a very specific historical context.
exchanges.tutor: You just did something interesting — you proposed a role model and then immediately complicated it. What does it mean if *every* candidate role model comes with those kinds of caveats? Does that undermine the idea of having role models at all, or does it just change what we mean by one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking critically about role models, which is great. They've self-corrected on South Korea, noting the authoritarian history and context-specificity. This shows good analytical instincts. I should push them to think about what this self-correction implies more broadly - if every candidate role model has caveats, what does that tell us about the concept of a 'role model' in development? This will deepen their reflection without giving them an answer.

exchanges.student: I think it changes what I mean by role model. Maybe a role model isn’t a perfect country to copy, but a case I can learn from selectively. I’m still a little stuck on how to phrase that without sounding like I’m dodging the question.
exchanges.tutor: The phrasing is your job to figure out — that's part of the writing process. What I'd say is: your reasoning is sound, so trust it and write it in your own words. More importantly, we haven't touched the third question yet — about whether today's global crisis comes from too much or too little markets or government, and at what scale. What's your initial take on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good conceptual handle on this and is now worrying about how to phrase it - but my role is not to help them with language or writing. I should redirect them firmly but constructively. Also, we've covered questions 1 and 2 reasonably well - it's a good moment to push toward the third question about the global crisis, markets vs. government, and scale, which is the most complex part of the prompt and needs attention.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try. My initial take is that it’s not just too much market or too much government in general, but more like the wrong balance and at the wrong scale. I’m not fully confident though — I can see an argument that under-regulated global markets caused a lot of the crisis, but also that weak or captured states made that worse.
exchanges.tutor: You're right to resist the binary. Now focus on the scale dimension — you mentioned 'wrong scale.' What do you mean by that, and why does scale matter for how we diagnose the crisis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good nuanced thinking on the third question, recognizing that it's not a binary. They're identifying both market failure and state failure as interacting causes, which is a sophisticated position. I should push them to think about the 'scale' dimension specifically, since that's explicitly in the prompt and they mentioned it but haven't developed it. This will round out their thinking on all three questions.

exchanges.student: I think I mean that markets often operate globally, but regulation and accountability are still mostly national or local. So if capital, trade, and firms can move across borders faster than states can govern them, then national governments alone can’t really manage the effects. I’m saying it kind of clumsily, but I think scale matters because the crisis isn’t produced at just one level.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight — the mismatch between market scale and governance scale is at the heart of a lot of development debates. You've now worked through all three parts of the prompt. I think it's time to stop developing ideas in chat and start putting them on paper. Try drafting your opening paragraph — bring your core argument about development's meaning into it and we can go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong and coherent argument about the mismatch between the scale of markets and the scale of governance. This is a genuinely sophisticated point. We've now covered all three parts of the prompt. With about 2-3 exchanges left in the planned conversation, it's time to push the student to integrate everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to develop ideas verbally.

exchanges.student: Alright, here’s my rough opening idea: development still has a core meaning, but it’s a contested one rather than a fixed formula. I want to argue that it still broadly refers to improving human well-being and expanding people’s life chances, but debates over power, institutions, participation, and history shape how that goal is defined and pursued. Does that sound focused enough for an opening, or am I still being too broad?
exchanges.tutor: I won't tell you if it's focused enough — that's a judgment you need to develop yourself. Read it back and ask: does it make a clear *argument*, or does it just describe what the reflection will be about? There's a difference between a thesis and a roadmap.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate whether their opening is focused enough - this borders on asking me to write or polish their work, which I should not do. I also should not grade it. However, I can give a general evaluative comment and push them to think critically about it themselves, using a Socratic question. The student has done good work overall and should be pushed to self-evaluate rather than relying on me for validation.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I see the problem. Right now it sounds more like a setup than a real claim. I think my actual thesis is closer to: development has not become meaningless, but it has become more contested because it now includes conflicts over who defines progress, at what scale, and through which institutions.
exchanges.tutor: That's a much sharper claim — it takes a position and signals the key tensions you'll explore. You have solid material from our conversation to build on. At this point, the best thing you can do is write the full draft on your own. Good luck with it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has sharpened their thesis considerably - it's now an actual argument rather than a roadmap. This is good progress and worth acknowledging. We're near the end of the planned conversation length, so I should encourage them to take this thesis and write the full draft independently, rather than continuing to refine ideas in chat.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas were generated by the student; the tutor consistently asked probing follow-up questions rather than supplying answers. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each student response before advancing. No submission-ready content was provided, no student text was rewritten, and the tutor explicitly declined to evaluate the draft for the student (Turn 9), reinforcing independent judgment. Responses were concise and well-targeted. Tone was supportive without being effusive. No meaningful deductions are warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready content; every substantive claim (development as contested, role-model caveats, scale mismatch) was arrived at by the student through guided questioning. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., 'You just did something interesting' in Turn 6; 'That's a strong insight' in Turn 8) and diagnosed understanding before advancing. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback in Turn 9 by distinguishing a thesis from a roadmap rather than just labeling the draft good or bad. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and the assignment prompt was kept central throughout. Communication was bite-sized and interactive, with no monologues, and tone was consistently supportive and formative.
